name: Ceryneian Hind
description: The Ceryneian Hind was a mythical hind with golden antlers and brazen hooves, sacred to Artemis. It was incredibly swift, capable of outrunning any pursuer. Heracles was tasked with capturing it alive as his third labor, a task that proved exceptionally challenging due to the hind's speed and sacred status. He pursued it tirelessly for a full year, traversing across Greece, before finally managing to capture it. Some accounts say he weakened it with an arrow (without drawing blood) before capturing it, while others state he caught it while it slept or was crossing a river. The hind's capture was a significant feat, demonstrating Heracles' strength, endurance, and respect (or lack thereof) for the gods. Its golden antlers and brazen hooves set it apart from ordinary deer, marking it as a creature of divine origin and exceptional power. The pursuit and capture of the Ceryneian Hind highlight the challenges and moral complexities inherent in Heracles' labors. The creature was eventually returned to Artemis after being shown to Eurystheus.
subtitle: Golden-antlered, sacred, and incredibly swift.
appearance: The Ceryneian Hind is described as a beautiful deer with golden antlers and brazen hooves. Its coat is typically depicted as a light brown or golden color. Its eyes are said to gleam with an otherworldly light. The golden antlers are its most distinctive feature, radiating a sense of divine power and grace. The brazen hooves allow it to traverse any terrain with ease, contributing to its incredible speed and agility.
habitat: The Ceryneian Hind was said to roam the hills of Ceryneia in Arcadia, Greece.
behavior: The Ceryneian Hind was an elusive and solitary creature, fiercely protective of its freedom. It possessed an innate sense of danger and was extremely wary of humans. Due to its sacred status, it was not typically hunted, and its behavior reflected a sense of confidence and independence. It was incredibly swift, constantly moving and difficult to track. It was revered by Artemis, the goddess of the hunt, and its behavior reflected its divine connection.

keyMyths.title: The Third Labor of Heracles
keyMyths.description: Heracles was tasked with capturing the Ceryneian Hind alive as his third labor. He pursued it for a year before finally succeeding, demonstrating his incredible endurance and skill.

sources.author: Apollodorus
